item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how had i not upvoted Coyote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder why they haven't used a scranton reality anchor on this guy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would think that part of the containment procedure would be telling the inhabitants exactly what the entity accepts in trade for water. They're already witnessing a talking walking coyote preach at them, and they're going to be amnesticized afterwards, so it couldn't hurt.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - CROSSLINK PROPOSAL
item.content:encoded: This sounds like a good alternative. I'll simply do an inline link in the footnote where it says coyotes.

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - CROSSLINK PROPOSAL
item.content:encoded:
Personally I think this one needs a crosslink to SCP-1848: the other Coyote. Knowing him, both are the probably the real one, but even odds that it might be another trickster like Raven stealing his schick. Either way, its probably for the best that they don't know about each other. Or at least that they don't know that the Foundation knows that they know about each other.

(I swear someday I'm going to figure out how to write a tale about these two if someone doesn't beat me to it.)

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - CROSSLINK PROPOSAL
item.content:encoded: That's a misinterpretation on my part, any recommendations on what I could crosslink to instead?

item.title: Re: STAFF POST - CROSSLINK PROPOSAL
item.content:encoded: Actually, the 5760 anomaly is related to SCP-3951, not 2547.

item.title: STAFF POST - CROSSLINK PROPOSAL
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reasoning
Hide Reasoning

Crosslinking to a related tale series.

Editing this:

To this:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot! Coyote is very spooky. Is making water dry up a thing he does in the myths?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are a wonderful person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I'd seen artwork of this SCP before I ever got around to reading it. Definitely fun, though I do wonder if the Foundation could do better to contain this. We are talking about a spirit out of myth and legend though, so it is gonna be an uphill battle.

Also, I used my free time and photoshop skills to make a "Kinky Coyote" advice animal template. :U For better or worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You said it yourself: this is a myth they’re dealing with. In general, you’re going to have a hard time containing those with black ops raids and trackers and titanium. It’s like using a sledgehammer to fix typos in a Google Doc. You make no progress and destroy the work you already had. The foundation has survived this long because they know how to speak the language of SCPs like this one.

They’re not appeasing it for the sake of appeasing it, but because doing so is part of how one interacts with this god: bartering, backstabbing, and generally tricking and being tricked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how this SCP is written. It has a tone that comes across as surreal and predatory, albeit somewhat uncontrolled. Therein lies my issue with it. It feels like there is little to nothing secured, contained, or protected in this.

I'm usually wary when I realize an SCP is drawing pointedly from real world lores/myths/religions. Many do a good job of applying the Foundation's cold, clinical tone to the SCP. Infiltrating an instance and trying to gain information is a good first Foundation-like step, but then they just try to appease it? Where's the D-Class personnel with sub-cutaneous trackers being sent in so even if they turn into a doge, they can still be tracked?

Also, if they're dousing each town in amnestics, you'd think 2547-1 would notice pretty quick and attempt to retaliate. That seems like something that should be at least hinted at.

So, again, it's not a bad SCP and I'm not downvoting. It feels like it's not finished, though, so no upvote either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I used it here because the sexual nature of the Coyote figure in mythology is very much part of his overall archetype, and it would have been ignoring a large facet of his mythology.

It would be like ignoring the fact that Zeus couldn't keep it in his pants and as such caused about 82% of all conflict in Ancient Greek mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well written, it's works with only a basic knowledge of the Coyote myths, and it seems faithful enough to the source without being a direct copy, but…

I'm mostly with Kalinin on his two main criticisms: "X myth is real!" has been done enough that I need more to justify an upvote, and I strongly prefer to keep sexual kinks, fetishes, etc. out of articles unless they are absolutely central to the premise. Consider this a figurative neutral-vote, good implementation balanced against dislike of some choices.

item.title: From #site19, with love
item.content:encoded:
furry joke

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp. That's my first tale of 2017.

Petrograd ilu

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Open the collapsible in the article and find this excerpt.

SCP-2547-1 will accept the following as payment: any kind of meat, pepper, flint arrowheads, knives, whips, leather, burlap, belts, the thorns of a saguaro cactus, broken glass, lost teeth, ties, carved sculptures, the corpses of domestic cats, amber, canvas shoes, sexual favors, sulfur, men's button-up shirts, animal skulls, and stories with SCP-2547-1 as the protagonist.2

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is the second footnote supposed to be on? I can't seem to find it, and I haven't been able to guess where it's supposed to be attached to

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for taking the time to read and comment - I very much appreciate your feedback! I'll keep this in mind for the next time I write. Thanks again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here because I was specifically asked for critique on this.

I went into this with a couple of factors that negatively affected my attitude toward this, one personal and one more applicable to how writing should be approached on the site. The personal one is the wink-wink-nudge-nudge pushing of yet more furry content on the site, which I don't enjoy and will downvote based on personal preference most of the time when I see it. I also don't enjoy the skipification of existing myths and legends. To me, the site is at its best when it's hitting that sense of the uncanny, when the reader is made to question what they know and don't know about their own sense of reality. The well-trod "what if [INSERT MYTH HERE] was real" prompt is too familiar for me to get that sense, and I've never found it all that imaginative or compelling a concept in fantasy writing, here and elsewhere.

Those cast a sort of negative tinge on the piece for me, but what really sealed my vote was how Coyote was portrayed. I don't mind the general idea of a prehistorical, "first god" type entity returning to the modern world and clashing with our supposedly more developed theologies, and in fact that could be very interesting. I guess I expected an entity like that to be a little more inscrutable and mystical than the character in this article. Coyote switches between highfalutin, vaguely pretentious religious pronouncements that are filtered through monotheistic religion and thus taking on its character, much in the way LaVeyan Satanism is just another flavor of Abrahamic religion, and this campy "randy old queen" persona which don't seem to mesh in any way. This dynamic, coupled with the fact that it's an animal that wants to fuck people1, are really preventing me from reading this as a singular, identifiable character and thus being invested in the main part of the narrative.

Is this a bad article? No, though in my reading it's definitely a flawed one. Even removing my personal preferences from the equation, I feel like the portrayal of Coyote was a missed opportunity to make a truly memorable, otherworldly character.

Footnotes
1. Yes, I'm aware that there's a far older basis for this attribute, but it's impossible to ignore the modern sensibilities that are being imposed here.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
You did it, AN.

You're officially a cool kid now.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Ayyyyy nevermind, time to party~
ayy lmao

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ding ding ding ding

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May I hazard a guest that the artist is Justin Vernon of Bon Iver and Kanye West?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you sure you got the frontman name's letters right? I'm 100% sure you might be missing a box there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're a fan of Gunnerkigg Court, this may feel a little too close to his Coyote for your tastes. There's also a couple of novels that do something similar with the character, as mentioned above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes. Most definitely yes.

The Foundation has no idea what they've gotten themselves into.

One DEFINITELY comes looking for poor Agent Miller.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I spent 5 minutes trying to figure out what I was missing that kept me from upvoting, and I'm still not sure. I know it's the Coyote, I get what he's doing but….

Maybe it's because I've read something like this before? Novoting, and I'll read it again later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He probably just got tired of hitting up Sloth's Pit, WI every year after realizing they were taking him for granted.

+1 in any case, this was a fun one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay! I guess I'm smarter than I give myself credit for. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oop

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (pst i'm not sure you actually upvoted sorry bluh)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of American Gods in a good way. I really like any fiction that has gods trying to return to prominence, and Small Town Furry Evangelist was a take I didn't expect to enjoy this much.

+1, for someone who claims not to be a furry you sure do write some entertaining anthropomorphic creatures, clearly you've wasted your talents on the wrong fandoms :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you're telling me that furry smut is how they keep an old trickster god in check?

Just asking for a friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a novel in the 'Company' series, by Kage Baker, titled "Sky Coyote" (might be Uncle Sky Coyote), that explores the mythology rather nicely.
Also a novel by Christopher Moore, "Coyote Blues", that explores it a bit more nastily.

Trickster Coyote is an inspiring entity, with or without his penis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd actually assumed that he was doing the prayer position but clasping a pipe to his chest instead of a crucifix…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in some stories, his penis would detach while he was sleeping and go have raunchy adventures of its own.

are you telling me that while the foundation is dealing with this property of Coyote, there might be a wandering penis with a bunch of other penises causing mayhem elsewhere

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I talk with Coyote all the time, and he says he did this stuff. -Crow Far-Walker

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is, in fact, THE coyote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So is this just the Coyote? I admit to being a tad dense, but I felt like I couldn't quite grasp who this was supposed to be at the end. And that seems like such a straightforward explanation, I wanted to check.

SCP fandom best start making Kinky Coyote reaction images, is all I'm sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TFW your research pays off in spades~

Fun fact about the figure of coyote from Native American myth: in some stories, his penis would detach while he was sleeping and go have raunchy adventures of its own.

I'm glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely wonderful. And, at least with what I know from my boyfriend, the aspect of the return to prominence is accurate to a lot of Native myth as well.

item.title: Wow. Coyote's a bit of a dick.
item.content:encoded:
I only vaguely recall this when I saw it as a draft, but I do remember that there was not an interview or an excerpt from one of Coyote's speeches, which to me is what really adds the flavor of this article and places it firmly in upvote land.

Between this article, and SCP-1160, it seems the Foundation has quite a few anomalies that are better contained the more people know about them, which is a rather refreshing reprieve from the usual Super Secret (tm) approach the Foundation usually applies.

+1. I dig it. Its a fun article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's so funny that you think they're related - Jekeled was actually looking over an early draft of this, and that's where he came up with the idea, after he suggested that maybe instead of just stealing water, the water turns into dogs. I passed on the idea because it was a little too cutesy-surreal for the "desert church Old Wild West fuckery" aesthetic I was going for, but he ended up making 2947 while I made this.

Also, I did mean to add something about amnestics in there. Not sure how that got past the multiple reviewers…

Weird!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only issue is how veil-shatteringly big this seems. Towns are being routinely hijacked by something blatantly anomalous, and the SCProcedures are a bit underwhelming (even considering the awesome grassroots-protocol). I have no idea how Secrecy comes into play here. This might be alleviated if it were explicitly set in a broken-veil scenario, ala Jekeled's recent thirst-doggos…in fact, I now headcannon these as related… 0-0

Excellent work on the charismatic Reverend, though. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As if the site wasn't already full of furries, you give us this. Have you no shame?

Well, I certainly don't. This is pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woops, that was meant to be the other way around. Thanks for the catch. fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A common street art design depicting a coyote wearing a leather jacket and sunglasses, using a pipe as a crucifix.

I'm having trouble visualising this. I'm not sure how one "uses" a crucifix, other than for crucifying people or warding off vampires, and I'm not sure how a pipe could be used in a way that makes it obvious from a still image that it is being used in place of a crucifix (other than perhaps crucifying someone on it). Clarification please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was as shocked as you are, honestly! I've always loved the figure of Coyote, and I love modern mythology, and I loved the aesthetic of this strange dog-man prowling these dusty little towns, trying to use the religions that have replaced him to get through to these terrified people.

And I love the use of narrative and creation as a containment method for skips (as seen in 2916) and I really wanted to think outside the box here. I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See I was wondering how this managed to hit +10 in the ten minutes before I got to it, but then I read it and I can definitely see why. It's cool to see a modern implementation of a mythological figure that shows it adapting to its present circumstances, especially one that works so well with such a dumb punny title.

… and admittedly, I have a weakness for anything that talks about advice animals, especially Foundation-sponsored advice animals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because I never want you to think about furries the same way again. Bonus points to anyone who guesses the artists who created the album…

Thanks to Magnus, AidenEldritch, SpectralDragon, vezaz, xeno, Gaffney, Rimple, Jekeled, minmin, and everyone else who looked at this in chat.

EDIT: And it's at 100, get hype!
